question: Define the Receiver Operator Characteristic (ROC) curve and state the two metrics that form its axes.
expectedPoints: The ROC curve is a graphical plot that illustrates the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier system.
The x-axis represents the False Positive Rate (FPR), calculated as FPR = FP / (FP + TN).
The y-axis represents the True Positive Rate (TPR), also known as recall or sensitivity, calculated as TPR = TP / (TP + FN).

question: Explain how the Area Under the Curve (AUC) score is interpreted and what it indicates about a model's performance. Describe what the value represents and what a score of 0.5 and 1.0 mean.
expectedPoints: The AUC provides an aggregate measure of performance across all possible classification thresholds.
It represents the probability that the model will rank a randomly chosen positive instance higher than a randomly chosen negative instance.
An AUC of 0.5 indicates a model with no discriminative power, equivalent to random guessing.
An AUC of 1.0 represents a perfect classifier that achieves 100% true positives and 0% false positives at some threshold.
A higher AUC value (closer to 1.0) indicates better overall performance and the model's ability to separate the two classes.

question: Compare and contrast the utility of the ROC curve with a Precision-Recall (PR) curve. In which scenarios is one more informative than the other? Justify your answer by discussing the impact of class imbalance.
expectedPoints: The ROC curve plots TPR (Recall) vs. FPR, while the PR curve plots Precision vs. Recall.

ROC curves can be overly optimistic for imbalanced datasets because the FPR (x-axis) might remain low due to a large number of true negatives (TN), even if the model performs poorly on the minority class.
PR curves are more informative for imbalanced datasets because the precision metric is sensitive to the number of false positives (FP), which is a critical concern when the positive class is rare.

A large shift in the number of negative instances (the majority class) significantly affects the denominator of FPR but does not affect the PR curve, making PR curves a more reliable performance measure in such cases.

The choice depends on the problem: ROC is suitable when the cost of false positives and false negatives is roughly equal and the class distribution is balanced. PR is preferred when the positive class is the focus and the dataset is imbalanced.
